easy.companyDescription: A subscription entertainment company trying to move more of its customer experience online.
easy.macroContext: Broadband use, connected devices, and paid digital media habits are all expanding.
easy.situation: Can a newer delivery model rebuild trust quickly enough to justify rising content and product spending?
easy.longCase: If customers accept the newer model, recurring revenue can scale with broadband and connected-device adoption.
easy.shortCase: A recent trust shock can keep churn elevated while content commitments rise faster than the subscriber base.
setupHints: The old delivery model still contributes cash, but the newer model is where the growth case lives.

medium.companyDescription: A consumer-facing digital business trying to turn repeat use into durable revenue.
medium.macroContext: Online consumer services are testing which monetization models can hold up as choice expands.
medium.situation: Can more frequent use improve unit economics before the costs of attracting users and supporting growth rise further?
medium.longCase: If repeat activity supports better monetization, the existing user base could produce more revenue without proportional marketing spend.
medium.shortCase: If engagement proves promotional rather than durable, acquisition and operating costs could outrun revenue growth.

hard.macroContext: Online services are competing for repeat use and spending.
hard.situation: Can stronger repeat activity lift monetization before growth-related costs become harder to absorb?
hard.longCase: More frequent use could raise the value of the existing user base and spread platform costs across more activity.
hard.shortCase: If activity stalls, user-acquisition spending and operating commitments could keep the model from scaling efficiently.

reveal.shortText: That was Netflix. The market was skeptical, but streaming adoption accelerated.
reveal.funFact: The company's transition looked risky at the time, but it became one of the defining consumer-tech winners of the decade.
whyItMoved: Subscriber growth improved after the trust reset.
The company became a stronger platform business over time.

sources.label: Prototype sample seed
sources.notes: Placeholder sample content for Phase 3 pipeline; not production research.

review.reviewNotes: H032 rewrote Medium and Hard after H030: the fixed boom-collapse-recovery silhouette plus an engagement/retention and subscription-media frame made Netflix dominant. The new variants use broader consumer-platform repeat-use, monetization, and acquisition-cost tension so 2011-2012 digital casualties also fit. Medium peer set: Groupon/Zynga/Pandora/Yelp. Hard peer set: Groupon/Zynga/Pandora/Yelp/Angie's List. Easy and shared labels remain unchanged; Medium/Hard Gate 2 results were removed as stale for blind rejudge.

prohibited: consumer media recurring-billing plus 2012 engagement-recovery conjunction
subscription-media framing coupled with the 2012 engagement-recovery silhouette
retention language that confirms the silhouette-implied Netflix candidate
content-cost commitments paired with recurring consumer media
